The surgeon successfully clears some omental adhesions from the extended gallbladder, focusing on the topmost portion known as the fundus or head, and attempts to lift it.
The surgeon gently separates tissue planes on one side of the gallbladder, avoiding the application of force, as the laparoscopic approach allows the planes to open naturally.
The surgeon observes the separation of tissue planes and considers cutting or cauterizing under direct vision to clarify the anatomy.

The surgeon observes the gallbladder filled with pus and prepares to aspirate the contents using suction, ensuring cleanliness and soft tissue handling.
The gallbladder wall appears gangrenous with multiple pus points visible, indicating inflammation and infection.

The surgeon mobilizes the upper portion of the gallbladder, which has a very thin and rotten wall. Infected bile and mucus are visibly leaking out, and the surgical team aspirates these fluids from the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses a suction tip to gently pull and dissect the surrounding tissue, minimizing trauma. The momentum is pushed aside to reveal the gallbladder, with the large colon and duodenum visible below.
The surgeon uses a fundus grasper to grasp the gallbladder and gently pushes it back, adjusting the position for better access.

The surgeon reaches the base of the gallbladder, where its attachments to the common bile duct and common hepatic duct are visible. The blood supply is being secured and delineated, with the cystic artery being clipped. The surgeon is also dealing with some of the peritoneal coverage around the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to gently peel away the peritoneal coverings from the gallbladder, revealing its attachments. The coverings appear degraded, requiring delicate handling to avoid damaging the softer underlying structures.
The surgeon uses a Maryland curved dissector and an infundibulum holder to gently manipulate and dissect the heart mass pouch and infundibulum on the right side, left side, and below the gallbladder.

The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder from the liver bed, gently mobilizing it to prevent entry into the liver. Rotten tissue is removed to improve visibility and reduce slipperiness during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully separates the gallbladder from the liver, staying as close to the gallbladder as possible during the dissection.
The surgeon creates a posterior window to access the Calot's triangle, gradually opening the area to locate the cystic duct and cystic artery.

The surgeon has opened part of the Calot's triangle, revealing fascia likely due to infection. A good portion of the dissection has been completed, exposing what appears to be the cystic duct and cystic artery. The surgeon emphasizes the need for a critical view of safety to confirm these structures.

The surgeon exposes the critical view of safety, clearly visualizing the cystic duct, cystic artery, and common hepatic duct. The gallbladder is mobilized from the liver bed, revealing liver tissue. Dissection continues from above, moving downwards, with the instrument passing to the right side to clarify the cystic artery and surrounding tissues, including small lymph nodes.

The surgeon gently dissects around the soft lymph node tissue, exposing the capsule of the lymph node. The goal is to achieve a clear critical view of safety, ensuring that vital structures connecting the liver to the intestine are preserved.

The surgeon identifies the main bile duct, observing the right and left bile ducts emerging from the respective lobes of the liver and combining. The fascia or vein is visible, along with what appears to be an artery.
The surgeon performs suction in the area, revealing a swollen and rotten gallbladder with a visibly thickened peritoneum.

The surgeon identifies the vein and artery, with thick and rotten tissue visible between them. The common bile duct and common hepatic duct are clearly exposed, with the lower portion of fascia running over the top of the common hepatic duct. The anatomy is clearly visualized, ensuring no harm to the structures.

The surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct using a clip applicator, ensuring the tip is positioned correctly on the opposite side before pressing to secure the clips. The critical view of safety is visible, including the common bile duct, cystic duct, cystic artery, and surrounding fossa.
